sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE] The claw could find me more than anything.
Yeah.
So we went to Mauthausen.
I told you about Mauthausen.
In Mauthausen we-- in Mauthausen we work on the [NON-ENGLISH].
I told you this.
Yeah.
And now I think what is when they-- when they took us out from Mauthausen.
So I was sleeping on the ground, laying on the ground.
I didn't go for food because I was tired.
So a German policeman came and he told me, in German, [GERMAN].
So, and he told me to stay in the line.
I go in the line, I see people [INAUDIBLE].
So when you see somebody [INAUDIBLE], you know they take you to the crematorium or they shoot you.
So I came, it was people, boys.
It was young boys in this time.
And they told me, Nathan, they called me, [POLISH], in Polish, we going out from that hell.
They gave us uniforms.
And the next day they-- the next day, they took us to a place, a labor camp, St.
Valentin-- St.
Valentin.
And over there I was working in a factory on a lathe.
They were making tanks there.
On a lathe?
On a lathe, I was working there.
And coming home, start to work 6 o'clock in the morning till 6 o'clock in the evening.
And coming home from the-- coming home, you have to go like a soldier, straight, because, and the door was open in that camp.
Because we were walking maybe 15 minutes to the place of work from the place where you were sleeping, that camp, the eating and whatever they gave you there.
So you have to go straight.
So I was tired and without shoes I was going like this.
So they took out the shoes from me in [NON-ENGLISH] and they told me to run in snow on the appellplatz.
Naked?
Naked. 10 times.
So I-- I figured out I was going to run the whole night, and I lived through that hell.
But this was already-- yeah.
And then the next day you're going to work again until they-- it was already close to the end the war because the Americans and the Russians, they used to bomb that camp.
So at night, when they-- we used to wait only, I was happy that they were bombing this camp.
So they used to take us to a tunnel at night when there-- when there-- when there was an alarm when they start to bomb that camp.
They take out all the people because they need us for work, to the-- to the tunnels.
One time a bomb fell in the tunnel, the entrance and the exit from the tunnel was smashed completely.
You couldn't go out from the tunnel.
So we digged with hands and we made a hole smooth to go out from that camp-- from that camp.
So it was already bombing.
They bombing day and night-- the Russians and the Americans.
And they liquidated that camp, and they announced whoever no want to work, you should come out.
They're going to take us by bus.
They're going to take that people by bus.
It was the opposite way.
That people they registered that they can walk, they killed them all.
The rest when they said they want to walk, they took us by bus.
So-- and while-- while going by bus to that town, Ebensee, it was a camp in the mountains, in the woods.
While we were going in the buses, the airplanes came and they started to bomb.
So the SS what was watching us, they-- because this was in the woods, all over in the woods-- they left the bus on the street with us, and they run away in the woods to hide before the bombs.
They were so mixed up coming back from the-- from the woods, they left the machine guns there.
They didn't know what's going on with them already, the police.
They forgot.
They came without machine guns.
Then they ran back and they brought us to that camp, Ebensee.
This was already before the liberation.
This is in Austria too-- in Austria, too.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and then-- and then-- in Ebensee, we was-- I don't know.
I think this was they came-- we came in Ebensee in 19-- already in 1945, in the beginning of 1945.
And there we was-- I was working at the same thing, the same lathe that I was working in Mauthausen and St.
Valentin, that lathe was already moved all the things there already.
This was a camp in the woods that during the day.
But 1 o'clock, the sun came in like a little bit in that was in the woods.
Over there was-- the hunger was terrible, unbelievable.
They didn't get nothing to eat because they couldn't deliver even because any transport everything was smashed.
They have you something that was smashed pieces because from the bombing.
So over there it was the hunger terrible, I used to dig the walls.
When I work on a-- when I work-- work in a-- in a lathe-- I don't know.
When I work on a lathe--
I took them.
I took them.
Oh, yeah?
OK, the glasses, that's right.
Yeah, I took them so you wouldn't sit on them.
When I work on a lathe and a tool fell down from the lathe, any tool, I couldn't bend down and pick up the tool.
I was so weak already.
But they had automatic-- automatic cranes.
I pushed the button, the crane came, and he picked it up for me because I couldn't pick it up.
So I was working there-- I was working there till in May.
Yeah, it was-- this was the worst camp from all, the hunger.
So the only thing you did for food was scraping this paint?
No, they give you--
They give you, have a quart of water, potato peels-- potato peels and water, yeah, potato peels and water.
But I forgot to tell you, while I was working where I was-- oh, St.
Valentin.
Before I came to-- to-- yeah, before I came to Mauthausen, I used to work in a-- in a-- in a factory there too on a lathe.
So I had a foreman, an Austrian man, Leo Humpel.
And I had a little closet by my lathe.
When I came in the morning, I opened that closet.
It was boil-- broil a little bit, baked potatoes, he used to bring me, that German, Leo Humpel from-- from-- I know the others.
Steiner, was a very nice man, an Austrian.
And then when-- that camp, the last one, was Ebensee, was the worstest camp in the world.
The hunger.
The hunger is terrible.
Is-- is-- you have no idea.
Nobody should know about this, what hunger is.
You're going around, your mind is only in one direction, where to get something to eat, where to get something.
You didn't care nothing.
You didn't, you know, you-- so over there, May-- where was this?
Yeah, on May the 6, 3 o'clock in the afternoon, I was sitting or lying there down.
A Russian man comes in and he tells in the Russian language, [RUSSIAN].
The Americana-- American soldier-- no, no.
Americana-- the Americans are on the appellplatz.
They are in the camp already.
So we, everybody, whoever could still walk went out.
And it was in that camp was 12,000 people. 12,000 people, where do they run after things like this?
It was a bakery.
Every run-- everybody runs to steal a piece of bread because it was very-- the hunger was very-- very, very big hunger in this time.
So I run too for the-- for the bread.
But the adult people in this time, everybody was not human, like animal's hunger.
So they run.
So I got already a piece of bread.
But they're running.
By that running, I hardly stand on my legs, and I fall down.
And they run over me, and my brain still was working.
So I figure out, oh my god, I lived in that hell, now that animal is going to kill me over here.
[LAUGHS] So, and a Russian guy was running and I caught his pants like this, with this pile was he was running, and he-- and I stand up.
So when I stand up it was two days later.
Was-- it was terrible in that time because the Russians, they-- or the German police, whatever it was, they used to kill them.
It was terrible.
So I don't know, a week later being at that camp, I was walking on the street in that Ebensee, a little town.
I was going to the Germans for food, things like this, because even liberated we didn't have what to eat right away.
So we were talking Jewish to each other and that-- that paper, what I have from that American-- what he was there, what I was working by them-- American officer.
And he understand Jewish and he ask us, what to were one.
And the other two guys when I was walking with him, he took them to work in the kitchen, and he took me into the garage.
And I was working-- working in that garage.
This was already in 1944, but there still was a war with the Japans.
And then that column, that what I was working by then, they moved him to Japan.
In that camp at that time, he gave me a paper.
I told him, I want to go back to Poland.
I want to see if somebody lives from my family.
So he gave me a pay paid so that every American soldier truck should take me all the way to the Czechoslovakian border.
And I came to the Czechoslovakian border.
I crossed the border, and this time wasn't so hard to go from one country to the other.
And I came to Czechoslovakia.
And from Czechoslovakia, I smuggled to Poland.
And I came to Poland and I find out--
Well, you-- you got borders.
Most of those places, the borders are in the woods.
And they told us at what time the police, when they're changing from one shift to the other shift to the other.
And this time they stay inside.
So we-- we find out the time where to go.
You know-- you know, You find out from people where to go.
And I came to Poland and I find out that my family is completely gone.
And my-- my parents, nobody knows where they took them.
I think they were Treblinka, a camp where they used to take the people and kill them there.
My sister, she no want to live her-- her husband's parents, and she went with them.
My brother, they told me, I find-- I find a-- a man from the other town, Gorlitz, he was with my brother to the last time in-- how do you call that camp?
I forgot the camp already.
So three days after the war, he died.
When the war was finished, he was very hungry, and then he started to eat and he got Typhus.
And he died three days after the war.
And I came to that little town where I was born, and it was a family they was hiding by Polish people during the war.
The family that you were--
That lives already, after the war they was already in that town.
Because it was only two Jewish families, what they came back already before me.
Well, they lived through that war.
The war was finished, and they came back to that town.
She just died in Israel.
And I came to her, and it start already, she gave me.
And I met there a few people.
I have them on the pictures over here.
And I was by her and she gave me to eat and things like this.
And I was by her about two weeks.
And-- I, with another friend, I have them over here now by the name Getz-- Getz.
And we smuggled again.
He had in Czechoslovakia, he has an aunt Bardejov-- in Bardejov, Czechoslovakia.
And we smuggled to that Czechoslovakia again.
And from-- and we was there-- what was it?
Yeah.
And we was there another two weeks.
And we went from Czechoslovakia with a-- with a train to-- from that place-- from that place, this was Slovakia, to Prague.
Prague-- Prague is a big city in Czechoslovakia.
And in Prague we-- we was there how long?
Maybe two weeks.
And we smuggled into Bavaria, to Germany.
Also, everybody smuggled the borders.
And we came to Bavaria in Germany.
And in Germany, in that place, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was Jewish people came back and was formed a Jewish committee.
And the American people helped a little bit.
They sent-- oh, it was there UNRRA-- UNRRA, the Jewish organization UNRRA.
And I lived in that place from 1942 till 1948-- no, 1940-- yeah, from 1943 already.
I don't remember exactly.
I was for five years in that place.
And I-- I-- I remember the address.
I have a cousin in the United States by the name of [INAUDIBLE].
His mother and my mother, they were sisters.
And right-- somehow I find out his address from the United States and I wrote to him a letter.
And he's-- and in between, I got-- in 1948-- 1948 or 1949-- 1948 I married my wife in Germany and he sent us papers.
In 1949 I came to the United States.
Yeah.
Yeah.
From the time they took me out from the little town, Biecz, they didn't-- I didn't know what was going on with them and they didn't know what's going on with me.
I know only when I run away from-- from that lumber, from that run away-- run away when they took me to push the bus.
When I came back home then I find out that everything is finished, that nobody is alive.
I just know that I have a brother in [INAUDIBLE] in a-- in an ammunition factory too, yeah.
No, no.
I just knew over there because-- how do I-- oh, yeah.
I had a-- I know he is there because I-- I work with a-- with a-- with a Polish-- Polack, with a Polack, and he was working with a Polack.
And we used to be in contact through that Polish people.
I used to write to that Polack what he was working with him and he used to the write to that Polack what I was working with him.
It wasn't a Polack, it was a German.
I-- I remember still the others, that Polish man that was working with my brother, it was, his name was Bielowitski, [INAUDIBLE], block 18.
Something like this, I still remember.
Yeah.
Oh, I forgot to tell you, when I was in [INAUDIBLE], in that labor camp, before that Krakow-Plaszow concentration camp, it was once, the light came out that night in the camp.
Sabotage.
They take out the whole camp outside.
And they put them to 10 people, every 10th out for shooting, out for shooting.
God help, I was the 10th.
When they went for the police to shoot, the lights came out, the lights came back.
It was a short and it was a win.
Someplace a wire came out from one place and came back.
The light went out, that's all you have.
You have by the split of a second of your life.
You see, a lot of people-- a lot of people-- I was young so I could take it.
A lot of people there was older, they was in the camps, you couldn't no more to walk to work.
So you couldn't walk to work, couldn't walk to work so you're not-- they don't need you there.
They shot you for nothing.
So it's a lot of things.

For instance, when I-- oh, yeah, in that camp, in the [INAUDIBLE], it used to be-- they used to-- you used to go 6 o'clock in the morning to work, so they take you out from the-- from the camp 5 o'clock, stay outside.
[NON-ENGLISH], and it's snow and everything.
And it was a Jewish guy.
It was a lot of Jews they was worse than SS.
Some of them, they were-- This was the Jewish police?
The Jewish-- no, he was a block barber.
He used to-- you know what, he was a barber.
If you have hair in the middle, they make a line like this.
They call it-- the call it a [NON-ENGLISH] promenade.
That you should not run away, that they should know right away that you are a--
So he used to come to people in the morning, so you stay-- you have to stay straight.
And I was cold so I stand like this.
And he kicked me in the stomach, and he tells me of Polish language, [POLISH].
Maybe would you straighten out?
So I straighten out and I told him-- his name was Marian.
And I told him, Marian, that war is not going to be forever.
I'm going to live through that-- through that hell, and I catch you.
And God help, I was in Germany already after the war in Bamberg.
And it was a shul there, and we used to come together.
The people if they survived the war, they used to come back there and get together every day.
Once I was already in Poland, I came back.
And when I stayed there, I look, oh my god.
Marian came in, the guy who he kicks me and what I told him that I'm going to live through that, through hell.
And he was-- and he came with some friends, I know them.
They are from the same time that my wife is.
And I was-- I don't to kill him, but the satisfaction that I told him, I'm going to live through that hell.
And I catch you.
I go to him, Marian, you recognize me?
And he says, Fl-fl-fl-Fleisher.
I say, yes Fleisher.
Remember I told you.
I catch you.
And it was there, it was there.
It was some rough people from before the war, Jewish too.
And it was a guy by Lieberman.
And he came to me and says, Nathan, he was hitting you in the camps, he says yeah.
And he took a knife, he wants to kill him.
So I told him, Marian, I don't want to kill him.
The satisfaction by me is more than-- than-- than you would kill him.
Then they start to hit him.
Then the MP-- the Jewish police came in-- not the Jewish, the American MP.
He says, in the United States you cannot just hit somebody like this.
We-- we arrest him and make a court.
Whatever they decide to do him.
So they arrest him.
And later on they start to beg me, what are they going to kill him, are they going to put him to life in jail.
And I signed a paper that was meant for him from before the war.
They let him go.
He came to the United States and they-- he applied for citizen papers.
But they know everything what he was, that he was hitting people.
They didn't give him the citizen papers, but he got married over here, and he invited me to the marriage-- to the wedding.
I was there in this time already married too, but I didn't go.
Yeah, so this-- this story, you could tell stories about this for a month.
You could write a book what's going on because you don't remember exactly everything, of what it was.
But it was something.
You didn't-- you didn't think about nothing, just to how to survive, how to survive.
You-- you have to watch, you shouldn't do-- you shouldn't do something wrong because when they-- when you do something, they start to hit you.
They kill-- they hit you so long until you're dead.
So you have to watch every step.
You should--
What would be wrong?
If they give you food.
So the guy who gave out food was an SS man.
So you took your food, you finish it, and you go the second time.
He recognize you, that's all.
You don't need it no more.
He recognize that you came the second time.
He tell you, stay there, out.
Stay out there.
And they boom, boom, boom.
That's all.
So for nothing, for nothing they kill you, for nothing.
Well, for anything.
For instance, they came at night, 1 o'clock at night, they walk into-- into the camps.
So you have to know that he-- when he walks in, you shouldn't wake up, even if you don't sleep, just keep like this.
If you-- if you look, you wake up at him.
You have something on mind.
You want to run-- you run-- you want to run away.
So you have to know that things.
When they come, you have to know that about 1:00, 12:30, 1 o'clock, they come in with dogs, with the German shepherds.
So you know that if you're not sleeping, you sleep.
If you was awake, you're not sleeping.
You're awake.
You have something in your mind.
You have something in your mind.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yeah.
Yeah, we could talk.
You couldn't organize nothing.
You couldn't do nothing there.
You were watched every step.
You could run away if you were lucky, the same way when I run away.
But it was-- it was difficult, you see.
The Polish people, you see, the German.
When I was-- when I run away, if I want to hide some place, the German couldn't recognize me that I am a Jew.
Because I know the Polish language perfect, but the Polack recognized me.
And the Polish people, a lot of them was interested the Jews shouldn't live through that war because the Germans gave them away the Jewish properties, you understand?
So the Polack, most of the Polish people, they was no good.
The people, like when I was hiding, you could count them on the fingers.
Tell me about that family where you were hiding.
That family, their name was Bielowitski.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was a very-- he wasn't rich.
But very, very, very poor-- very, very good people.
So when I run away from that camp, Lemberg, Lwow, when I came back home, when I find nobody is alive, I went to him and he helped me and I was by him about two weeks sleeping on the attic.
And those-- those-- it's three girls was over there, yeah.
The children.
Two-- two are in Poland-- yeah.
Two are in Poland, and the one is in Canada.
I just have-- I just have a letter from Poland from Saturday.
If you want a letter with a picture, I could show it to you, a letter from her.
She is still in Poland.
There used to be one was 11 years old, the other one was eight years old.
And the other one, the older one, was working already in Germany because they took the Polish girls to work-- to work to Germany on the farms.
They got it good.
They work and they-- after the war, they came back.
So that two girls used to go stealing from other apples and fruit, things, to bring me up there in the attic-- on the attic to eat.
And it was-- this was very nice, very nice people.
They do everything I should be alive.
But I went-- they didn't tell me to go away.
I went away by myself, because it's a very small town.
I know this is young children.
No matter how smart she is, she's eight years old.
She could tell somebody, [POLISH], my Polish name, [POLISH] is hiding by.
So I know if they find out, they're going to kill the whole Polish family and me.
So I went by myself.
I went to Krakow.
Because I had, in this time, I know my-- my sister's husband was already in that camp in [INAUDIBLE], so I walk into that camp by myself.
